title: Source transparency and attribution
content: Credible reporting usually names the source of a claim, links to primary documents, includes dates, and distinguishes verified facts from speculation. Missing or vague attribution raises the chance that a statement is unsupported.

title: Clickbait headline patterns
content: Clickbait often uses shock language, withheld details, or exaggerated promises such as 'you will not believe', 'secret', or 'this changes everything'. The headline usually creates curiosity gaps that the body does not justify.

title: Emotional manipulation signals
content: Manipulative content pushes fear, outrage, guilt, or tribal loyalty to reduce careful reasoning. Intense emotional framing without concrete evidence is a common red flag.

title: Urgency and pressure tactics
content: Scams and deceptive campaigns often pressure users to act immediately with phrases like 'act now', 'limited time', 'before it is deleted', or 'within 24 hours'. Urgency is especially suspicious when combined with money requests or account warnings.

title: Financial scam indicators
content: High-risk financial scams frequently ask for bank details, one-time passwords, crypto transfers, gift cards, wallet recovery phrases, or upfront payments. Promises of fast returns, guaranteed profits, or unexpected prizes are major risk indicators.

title: Impersonation and domain spoofing
content: Fraudulent pages may imitate trusted brands with lookalike domains, copied logos, and language claiming official status. Verification should include checking the exact domain, official contact channels, and whether the request appears on the real organization's site.

title: Missing context in statistics and quotes
content: Numbers, polls, and quotes can mislead when they lack methodology, sample size, original publication date, or surrounding context. Trust increases when statistics can be traced to a primary study or official dataset.

title: Verification workflow
content: A strong verification process compares claims with primary sources, checks whether multiple independent outlets agree, inspects author and publication credibility, and separates fact reporting from opinion or sponsored material.

title: Traits of credible reporting
content: More trustworthy content tends to use measured language, identifiable authors, direct evidence, balanced framing, and verifiable references. It avoids absolute certainty when facts are incomplete.
